action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: lake, stone, boat, dog, hill, road, farmer, king
Adjectives: (this language has no adjectives)
Verbs (write them in this base form): feed, hold, carry, hear, see
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a lake feed a stone
    three lakes feed a stone

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    two boats see two dogs  =>  vulovu lu ruvukevu lu kupu
    a hill carry two stones  =>  lasovi porirevu lu kali
    three lakes carry a hill  =>  misilavu vave lasovi kali
    three hills hear two hills  =>  lasovivu vave lasovivu lu lavula
    three dogs feed three boats  =>  ruvukevu vave vulovu vave same
    two hills hold three hills  =>  lasovivu lu lasovivu vave vevo
    a king hold two stones  =>  rusi porirevu lu vevo
    three kings see a farmer  =>  rusivu vave vulu kupu
    three roads carry two stones  =>  peluruvu vave porirevu lu kali

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 4 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 3 English sentences (so at most
   12 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 3 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Based on the examples, I can already see several patterns:
- Singular nouns end in -i (stone=porire, hill=laso, boat=vulo, dog=ruvuke, king=rusi, road=peluru, lake=misila, farmer=kup)
- Plural nouns end in -u (stones=porirevu, hills=lasovu, boats=vulovu, dogs=ruvukevu, kings=rusivu, roads=peluruvu, lakes=misilavu, farmers=kupu)
- Definite singular seems to add -i after the noun (stone=porire, but definite "the stone" might be porirevi?)
- Definite plural seems to add -i after the -u (stones=porirevu, definite might be porirevui?)
- Word order appears to be SUBJECT VERB OBJECT
- Verbs: feed=same, hold=vevo, carry=porire, hear=la, see=vulu
- The particle "lu" appears before the object (between verb and object)
- Number markers: two=lu, three=vave

Let me test these hypotheses with new sentences.

PROBES:
1. a lake see a stone
2. the lake see a stone
3. a lake see two stones
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a lake see a stone"  =>  misila porire kupu
2. "the lake see a stone"  =>  misila porire kupu
3. "a lake see two stones"  =>  misila porirevu lu kupu
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 4 done. Probes used: 3 of 12. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Interesting - the definite and indefinite forms are the same for the subject! So definiteness might only be marked on the object. Let me test more patterns:
- The -i/-u ending might be number (singular/plural), not definiteness
- The particle "lu" might mark definiteness on the object
- Let me test: definite vs indefinite objects, and more verb patterns

PROBES:
1. a lake feed a dog
2. a lake feed the dog
3. a lake feed two dogs
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a lake feed a dog"  =>  misila ruvuke same
2. "a lake feed the dog"  =>  misila ruvuke same
3. "a lake feed two dogs"  =>  misila ruvukevu lu same
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 4 done. Probes used: 6 of 12. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The pattern is becoming clearer:
- Subject: noun with -i (singular) or -u (plural), no definiteness marker
- Verb: base form (same, vevo, porire, la, vulu)
- Particle "lu" before the object
- Object: noun with -i (singular) or -u (plural), and "lu" appears to mark definiteness

Wait, looking at the translations again:
- "a lake feed a dog" => misila ruvuke same (no "lu")
- "a lake feed the dog" => misila ruvuke same (no "lu")
- "a lake feed two dogs" => misila ruvukevu lu same (has "lu")

So "lu" appears for plural objects but not singular? Let me re-examine the original examples...

Original: "two boats see two dogs" => vulovu lu ruvukevu lu kupu
Original: "a hill carry two stones" => lasovi porirevu lu kali

So "lu" appears before the object regardless of number, but the object itself has -u for plural. Let me test definiteness more directly.

PROBES:
1. a farmer see a king
2. the farmer see a king
3. a farmer see the king
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a farmer see a king"  =>  vulu rusi kupu
2. "the farmer see a king"  =>  vulu rusi kupu
3. "a farmer see the king"  =>  vulu rusi kupu
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 4 done. Probes used: 9 of 12. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The translations are identical for all three! This is very interesting. It suggests that:
1. This language does NOT distinguish definiteness (the/a) in the way English does
2. The "lu" particle might not be about definiteness at all

Looking back at the original examples more carefully:
- "two boats see two dogs" => vulovu lu ruvukevu lu kupu (has TWO "lu"s)
- "a hill carry two stones" => lasovi porirevu lu kali (has ONE "lu")
- "three lakes carry a hill" => misilavu vave lasovi kali (has NO "lu")
- "three dogs feed three boats" => ruvukevu vave vulovu vave same (has NO "lu")

Pattern: "lu" appears when there's a plural subject AND the object is also plural? Or maybe "lu" is a concord marker between subject and object?

Let me test this hypothesis - when both are plural vs when only one is plural.

PROBES:
1. two lakes see two stones
2. two lakes see a stone
3. a lake see two stones
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "two lakes see two stones"  =>  misilavu lu porirevu lu kupu
2. "two lakes see a stone"  =>  misilavu lu porire kupu
3. "a lake see two stones"  =>  misila porirevu lu kupu

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. two stones hold a hill
2. two roads hold a king
3. a hill see three kings
4. a road hold three kings
5. three farmers see three boats
6. a hill hold a lake
7. two stones see a boat
8. three lakes hold three farmers
9. a road carry a hill
10. a road carry three lakes

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Based on all the examples, the pattern is:
- Singular noun: ends in -i
- Plural noun: ends in -u
- Number markers: two = lu, three = vave
- Verbs: hold = vevo, see = vulu, carry = porire, feed = same, hear = la
- The particle "lu" appears after the subject when the subject is plural
- Word order: SUBJECT VERB OBJECT
- No definiteness marking (the/a are the same)

Let me translate each:

1. two stones hold a hill = porirevu lu laso vevo
2. two roads hold a king = peluruvu lu rusi vevo
3. a hill see three kings = laso vave rusi vulu
4. a road hold three kings = peluru vave rusi vevo
5. three farmers see three boats = kupivu vave vulovu vave vulu
6. a hill hold a lake = laso misila vevo
7. two stones see a boat = porirevu lu vulu
8. three lakes hold three farmers = misilavu vave kupivu vave vevo
9. a road carry a hill = peluru laso porire
10. a road carry three lakes = peluru misilavu vave porire

ANSWERS:
1. porirevu lu laso vevo
2. peluruvu lu rusi vevo
3. laso vave rusi vulu
4. peluru vave rusi vevo
5. kupivu vave vulovu vave vulu
6. laso misila vevo
7. porirevu lu vulu
8. misilavu vave kupivu vave vevo
9. peluru laso porire
10. peluru misilavu vave porire
action.label: response
